title: Native addon linking (bare-link) silently breaks for any consumer outside this exact monorepo -- 'one-line install' is broken today
description:
CONTEXT: discovered while building E9.3 (flutter_pear-df9.3, fresh-machine CI job). flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle's linkNativeAddons task locates pear-end/node_modules via 'def pearEndDir = new File(rootDir, "../../flutter_pear/pear-end")' where rootDir is the CONSUMING APP's android/ directory (Gradle's rootDir always refers to the overall build's root, i.e. the app, not the plugin's own directory -- confirmed empirically, not just by reading the code). This relative path only resolves correctly when the consuming app itself happens to live at <some-monorepo>/packages/<app-name>/, sibling to packages/flutter_pear -- i.e., ONLY flutter_pear_example, the one app that happens to live inside this exact monorepo. The code's own comment already flags this as a known risk ('a real external consumer... would resolve this wrong') but it had never actually been empirically verified broken until now.

REPRO (this session): created a brand-new 'flutter create fresh_app' OUTSIDE this repo (in a scratch dir), added flutter_pear as a path dependency pointing at the real packages/flutter_pear checkout (flutter_pear's own pubspec.yaml correctly path-deps on ../flutter_pear_bare, which resolves fine as a Dart/pub path -- the bug is Gradle-side, not pub-side). Built successfully (no Gradle error -- onlyIf silently skips the task instead of failing loud) but the resulting APK is missing EVERY linked native addon .so file: no libudx-native, libsodium-native, libbare-fs, libbare-os, libbare-type, libbare-url, libbare-inspect, libfs-native-extensions, libquickbit-native, librabin-native, librocksdb-native, libsimdle-native -- only the base libbare-kit.so shipped. Installing and launching the app crashes immediately: 'AddonError: ADDON_NOT_FOUND: Cannot find addon .  imported from .../udx-native/binding.js', SIGABRT in the bare-worklet thread. Confirmed NOT a df9.1 dependency-pin regression: rebuilt flutter_pear_example itself from a fully clean state (flutter clean, fresh build/) on the same emulator and it built+ran fine -- the difference is purely that flutter_pear_example happens to live inside this monorepo where the hardcoded relative path accidentally resolves.

IMPACT: this breaks CLAUDE.md's own non-negotiable 'One-line install' convention ('flutter pub add flutter_pear and it builds -- native Bare Kit binaries + pear-end resolve via Gradle task... Zero manual NDK/ABI/Podfile edits') for ANY real consumer, not just post-pub.dev-publish -- even TODAY's documented pre-publish git-dependency install method (README's 'Install' section: git: {url, path: packages/flutter_pear, ref}) would hit this exact bug for any app that isn't itself inside a directory structure mimicking this monorepo's layout, since pub's git-dependency cache is a full clone but the CONSUMING APP is the developer's own project, elsewhere entirely. This also calls into question E4's 'Native distribution' epic being marked complete -- the Gradle fetch/link wiring works for Bare Kit's own prebuilt binaries (fetched by version, no directory-relative hack) but NOT for pear-end's native addon linking step, which is architecturally different and untested outside this monorepo until now.

LIKELY FIX DIRECTION (not yet implemented, needs its own design/ticket): stop depending on a live pear-end/node_modules at CONSUMER build time entirely -- pre-extract the linked .so files at PACK TIME (dart run flutter_pear:pack, which already runs bare-pack for the JS bundle and the license collector) and commit them into flutter_pear_bare/android/src/main/jniLibs/<abi>/*.so as versioned, committed artifacts -- exactly the same pattern already established for assets/pear-end.bundle and THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES (prebuilt once, committed, never regenerated at consumer build time). Android's Gradle Plugin auto-includes src/<variant>/jniLibs/<abi>/ with zero custom task wiring, which would ALSO delete the fragile rootDir-relative-path hack entirely rather than just relocating it. This would need: extending bin/pack.dart to invoke bare-link and copy outputs; simplifying/removing linkNativeAddons's Exec task in favor of the standard jniLibs convention; committing the resulting per-ABI .so files; updating LICENSING.md/CLAUDE.md's 'committed, versioned artifact' language to cover these binaries too; re-verifying with a genuine outside-the-monorepo consumer app (exactly the fresh_app repro above) as the closing validation.
design: See full analysis in the description. Root cause confirmed via bytewise .so manifest diff (unzip -l) between a clean flutter_pear_example build and a fresh external consumer app build on the same emulator/toolchain, plus logcat crash evidence (ADDON_NOT_FOUND, SIGABRT in bare-worklet thread).
acceptance_criteria: A 'flutter create'-generated app living OUTSIDE this monorepo, with flutter_pear added as a path or git dependency, builds an APK containing every linked native addon .so (matching flutter_pear_example's own current .so manifest) and Pear.start() completes without an AddonError/SIGABRT crash. The onlyIf silent-skip in linkNativeAddons either no longer exists (fixed architecture) or is replaced with a loud failure if the fallback path is ever taken.
close_reason:
Root cause: flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle's linkNativeAddons Gradle Exec task ran bare-link against pear-end/node_modules AT A CONSUMER'S OWN BUILD TIME, located via a path relative to the CONSUMING APP's android/ directory -- only ever correct for flutter_pear_example (which happens to live inside this monorepo). Any real external consumer's onlyIf guard silently skipped linking with no error, shipping an APK missing every native addon; Pear.start() crashed instantly (AddonError: ADDON_NOT_FOUND, SIGABRT in bare-worklet).

FIX: moved bare-link to PACK time (dart run flutter_pear:pack's new linkNativeAddons in bin/pack.dart, called right after buildBundle). Commits the resulting per-ABI .so files (arm64-v8a, x86_64) as versioned artifacts under flutter_pear_bare/android/src/main/jniLibs/<abi>/ -- Android's standard jniLibs convention picks these up at any consumer's build time with zero custom Gradle task, matching the same 'committed, versioned artifact' treatment assets/pear-end.bundle already gets. Removed the old linkNativeAddons Exec task, its onlyIf guard, pearEndDir/nativeAddonsDir vars, and related wiring from build.gradle entirely; fetchBareKit (Bare Kit's own runtime binary fetch, unrelated/still correct) is untouched.

VERIFIED end-to-end: rebuilt flutter_pear_example from clean -- byte-identical .so manifest before/after, builds and runs with no crash. Rebuilt a genuine 'flutter create'-outside-this-repo consumer app with flutter_pear as a path dependency -- APK now contains every native addon .so (previously zero), installs, launches, and Pear.start() completes with a logged+screenshotted 'worklet attached' confirmation (previously crashed within ~1s of launch).

Code review (medium effort) on the first draft caught two real gaps, both fixed: (1) the destination jniLibs dir was wiped before verifying bare-link actually produced output for every ABI -- reordered so every ABI's .so list is verified non-empty in a scratch dir BEFORE any destructive write touches the committed directory, and a short-of-every-ABI run now fails loud instead of silently shipping a partial result; (2) no pruning of stale ABI directories if the ABI list ever shrinks (comment claimed parity with fetchBareKit's own pruning, which wasn't true) -- added the same sweep-and-delete fetchBareKit already does, plus a new test proving it. Also switched from --preset android (links 4 ABIs) to explicit --host android-arm64/android-x64 (links only the 2 this repo ships) for less wasted work, and added a test asserting bin/pack.dart's nativeAddonAbis stays in sync with build.gradle's bareKitAbis (previously linked only by a comment, with no enforcement).

16 pack_test.dart tests (up from 9), all green; melos analyze and melos test (193 cases) both green. The ~27MB of new .so files are explicitly git-added (staged, not committed per this repo's manual-commit policy) so they can't be accidentally missed at commit time -- code review specifically flagged this as a real risk (a git status review that only looks at modified-tracked-files misses a wholly new untracked directory). Noted on flutter_pear-df9.4 (device/ABI/release matrix CI): today's ci.yml never builds a real APK, so this fix's own test always skips on the hosted runner and there's no automated backstop yet for this path -- flagged for that ticket to close when it wires a real build+run job.

title: E4.4: Harden worklet into the full RPC bridge (make the fakes real)
description:
CONTEXT: E4, plan step 3a. Harden the E1 gate spike into the full RPC bridge: the worklet must implement the complete E2 schema so the fakes' behavior becomes real.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js (+ modules), FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt touch-ups.
STEPS:
1. Implement the full schema in pear-end: attach handshake (nonce+bundleVersion from E2.5), discriminator framing (E2.4), err.code registry usage (E2.3), X8 state events (E2.7), dynamic topic join/leave (replace E1.4's hardcoded topic).
2. File-path bulk seam (locked, codex #4): expose a worklet op that writes received bulk payloads to an app-storage file and returns the path — the primitive Hyperdrive (E5) will use. NO in-channel chunking/streaming.
3. Rebuild bundle; bump bundle version.
VALIDATION: the flutter_pear spine test suite (E2.2-E2.7) passes against the REAL worklet on a device/emulator (same tests that pass against the fake — this is the fake-vs-real drift check); two-device chat still works.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: pear-end implements the complete E2 schema (handshake, framing, err registry, state events, dynamic topics) + file-path bulk seam; the E2 spine tests pass against the real worklet on hardware, matching fake results.
notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review (8 numbered checks, read the installed hyperswarm source and traced the framing accumulator byte-by-byte on both Dart and JS sides for 4 edge cases -- single frame, split length-prefix, incomplete payload, zero-length frame) found zero P1s. Two low-priority, non-blocking concerns filed as flutter_pear-pcg: (1) BULK_STORAGE_DIR's Bare.argv[0] has no explicit null-safety guard (currently unreachable in practice since every startWorklet() boot path always passes argv, but worth a defensive check), (2) pear-end has zero JS-level unit tests of its own -- E4.4's dynamic SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE logic is only indirectly exercised via swarm.dart's pre-existing fake-worklet tests (which never touch pear-end's real JS), a plain Node-run test stubbing Hyperswarm could close this gap without needing a device. Reviewer also independently confirmed swarm.dart needed NO changes for this ticket (its existing topic-hex event filtering, from E2.7, is already correctly compatible with pear-end's new multi-topic events) and confirmed SWARM_LEAVE's cleanup is graceful when a still-live connection's info.topics includes an already-left topic.
close_reason: pear-end now implements the complete E2 schema for real: dynamic Dart-driven multi-topic join/leave (replacing the E1 gate's hardcoded topic), plus the file-path bulk seam for E5's future Hyperdrive work. Along the way, discovered and fixed a critical pre-existing transport bug (IPC frame coalescing under burst traffic, present since E2.4) with symmetric length-prefix framing on both sides of the Dart/JS boundary, backed by 3 new dedicated tests. 48 automated tests pass across both packages, reviewed independently with zero P1s, and validated end-to-end on the Android emulator against the real worklet (dual simultaneous topic joins, independent bounded timeouts, and a byte-exact bulk file round trip). Two minor hardening follow-ups tracked in flutter_pear-pcg; two-device hardware validation remains deferred per standing project directive.

title: E4.3: INTERNET permission in library manifest, release-verified
description:
CONTEXT: E4, eng T6 (codex #8). The LIBRARY manifest declares no INTERNET permission; debug builds mask it, release builds fail networking silently in the field.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml
STEPS:
1. Add <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET"/> to the library manifest (manifest-merger propagates to apps).
2. Remove any spike-era permission from the example app manifest (E1.2) so the library manifest is proven to carry it alone.
VALIDATION: flutter build apk --release of the example; install the RELEASE apk on a device; swarm join succeeds (proves merged permission). aapt dump permissions on the release apk lists INTERNET.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Library manifest carries INTERNET; example app manifest carries no spike duplicate; RELEASE build joins a swarm on device; aapt dump shows the merged permission.
notes: RETROACTIVE CORRECTION (2026-07-03): this issue's on-device 'clean boot, no crash' checks never actually proved a real worklet->Dart byte round trip -- see flutter_pear-vyw.3 for the root cause (BinaryCodec platform-channel bug, present since E1.3, fixed in E2.5/flutter_pear-7be.5). The Dart-level unit tests and analyze/build validation for THIS issue's own logic remain valid and unaffected; only the on-device smoke-test claims are corrected.
close_reason:
Library manifest already carried INTERNET (added during E1.4) with a comment explaining the auto-merge; example app manifest already carried no duplicate. This task's job was validating that end-to-end on a RELEASE build, which surfaced a real, separate bug: libbare-kit.so aborts on boot in release mode ('JNI DETECTED ERROR IN APPLICATION: mid == null', from its MessageQueue.nativePollOnce integration) because R8 strips to.holepunch.bare.kit's unobfuscated JNI-called methods with no keep rule protecting them. Fixed via packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/consumer-rules.pro (-keep class to.holepunch.bare.kit.** { *; }) wired through consumerProguardFiles, so every consuming app gets it automatically -- no manual app-dev step.

VALIDATION (emulator, per developer decision to use emulator over BYD hardware): flutter build apk --release --target-platform android-arm64 succeeds; aapt dump permissions on the release APK lists android.permission.INTERNET; adb shell dumpsys package confirms INTERNET granted=true; installed + launched on Medium_Phone_API_35 -- before the proguard fix this crashed immediately (SIGABRT, full tombstone captured); after the fix, clean boot, no crash, 'Fully drawn', process stays alive. melos run analyze clean (3/3 packages).

title: E4.1: Production Gradle fetcher — checksum, ABI config, bundle install
description:
CONTEXT: E4. Productionize the E1.2 spike into real distribution: today build.gradle has no fetcher, checksum, ABI config, or bundle install (codex #7 called this more fundamental than RPC design). Mirror react-native-bare-kit's resolution approach.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle (+ optional buildSrc/gradle task file)
STEPS:
1. Gradle task resolving Bare Kit native artifacts for arm64-v8a AND x86_64 (emulator); make 32-bit an explicit include-or-exclude decision recorded in a comment.
2. SHA-256 checksum verification of every downloaded artifact against pinned values.
3. Install the pear-end bundle (from the pack output path) into assets as part of the build.
4. Pin the Bare Kit version in one place (feeds the compat table in E9).
VALIDATION: flutter build apk --debug and --release succeed on a clean checkout (cold Gradle cache); tampering one byte of a cached artifact fails the checksum check with a clear error.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Clean-checkout debug+release builds resolve Bare Kit for arm64+x86_64 with SHA-256 verification and install the pear-end bundle; corrupted artifact fails loud; version pinned in one place.
notes: RETROACTIVE CORRECTION (2026-07-03): this issue's on-device 'clean boot, no crash' checks never actually proved a real worklet->Dart byte round trip -- see flutter_pear-vyw.3 for the root cause (BinaryCodec platform-channel bug, present since E1.3, fixed in E2.5/flutter_pear-7be.5). The Dart-level unit tests and analyze/build validation for THIS issue's own logic remain valid and unaffected; only the on-device smoke-test claims are corrected.
close_reason:
Productionized the E1.2 spike's Gradle fetch/link tasks: 

1. SHA-256 checksum verification: bareKitSha256 pinned (independently verified by re-downloading the release asset to a separate location and re-hashing -- matched). verifySha256() runs unconditionally every fetchBareKit execution, before extraction. Real bug found and fixed mid-implementation: Gradle's incremental-build machinery marked fetchBareKit UP-TO-DATE once its outputs already existed, silently SKIPPING the checksum on every subsequent build -- confirmed by tampering one byte of the cached zip and watching the build succeed anyway. Fixed with outputs.upToDateWhen { false }, confirmed the same tamper now fails loud with the exact expected-vs-actual hash in a clear GradleException.

2. ABI matrix: bareKitAbis = [arm64-v8a, x86_64] (was arm64-v8a only). x86_64 covers desktop-class emulators (Apple Silicon's native-arm64 images already worked via arm64-v8a; this adds Intel/CI x86_64 support). armeabi-v7a/x86 (32-bit) explicitly excluded with a comment -- bare-kit's own minSdk 31 leaves no realistic 32-bit target regardless of distribution channel. Wired into both fetchBareKit's zip-extraction filter and linkNativeAddons's ABI-pruning filter (previously hardcoded to arm64-v8a in both places).

3. Version pinning: bareKitVersion was already single-sourced from E1.2; confirmed unchanged.

4. Bundle-into-assets: already handled by Flutter's own asset pipeline (pubspec.yaml's existing assets: declaration) -- no additional Gradle wiring needed; verified by inspection, not re-implemented.

REVIEW (independent agent) found no blocking bugs -- empirically re-verified the checksum hashing byte-for-byte (including the sign-extension hex-encoding gotcha and a non-8192-aligned final chunk) and confirmed upToDateWhen{false} doesn't cascade unnecessary downstream reruns (Gradle's own content-fingerprinting still short-circuits mergeJniLibFolders etc. since the re-extracted bytes are identical). Two minor nits applied: reworded the 32-bit-exclusion comment (led with minSdk 31, not Play Store policy, which doesn't actually mandate dropping 32-bit); added explicit ABI pruning to fetchBareKit's copy{} step to match linkNativeAddons's existing pattern (copy{} only adds, never removes stale entries -- closes a latent staleness gap if bareKitAbis is ever narrowed without a version bump).

VALIDATION: cold-cache clean build (deleted the whole bare-kit + bare-kit-addons build dirs) -- fresh download, checksum pass, both ABIs extracted/linked correctly. flutter build apk --debug and --release both succeed (release APK grew from ~83.7MB to ~153MB now bundling both ABIs in one universal APK -- ABI splitting to avoid that bloat is explicitly E4.5's job, not this ticket's). Tamper-one-byte test fails loud with a clear message; recovers cleanly once the bad file is removed. Normal warm-cache rebuild costs ~1.7s total (negligible re-verify overhead). Installed + launched both debug and release builds on the Android emulator -- clean boot, no crash, 'Fully drawn', both times.

title: E2.7: PearSwarm connection-state stream + honest failure (X8)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, DX X8 (locked D13). On UDP-blocked/carrier-NAT networks P2P can be impossible; today that is an infinite silent wait on swarm.connections. The API must be honest: state stream + bounded timeout + typed failure with WHY.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/swarm.dart, schema.dart/schema.js (state event + failure codes e.g. E_UDP_BLOCKED, E_CONNECT_TIMEOUT), pear-end/index.js (emit state transitions), test/swarm_test.dart (new, against mocks/fake).
STEPS:
1. Define states in schema: discovering, connecting, connected, reconnecting, failed.
2. pear-end emits state events from Hyperswarm lifecycle (join flushed, connection attempts, errors) with failure reason codes where detectable.
3. PearSwarm exposes Stream<PearSwarmState> state; join() gains a bounded discovery/connect timeout after which state=failed with typed reason + docs anchor string.
4. Failure codes registered in the E2.3 err registry so they render via the (later) error catalog.
5. Tests: state sequence on happy path; timeout path reaches failed with typed reason (never hangs); reconnect transition emitted.
VALIDATION: flutter test test/swarm_test.dart green; manual: airplane-mode/blocked network shows failed + reason within the timeout bound, no spinner.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: BROADCAST state stream emits discovering/connecting/connected/reconnecting/suspended/failed; bounded timeout guarantees failed+typed reason (E_UDP_BLOCKED class) instead of infinite wait; write-after-close fails with typed PearConnectionException (tested); codes in registry; timeout path tested.
notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review (8 numbered checks incl. reading the installed hyperswarm@4.17.0 source and running 2 targeted regressions to confirm tests catch what they claim) found zero P1s. Two low-severity CONCERNs fixed before close: (1) the leave()-cancels-the-timer test's sawFailed assertion was dead code -- a leaked timer would throw a StateError adding to the already-closed _state controller BEFORE ever reaching the listener, so the real protection was always the uncaught-error path, not the flag; rewrote the test to be honest about that mechanism (reaching the end of the test cleanly IS the assertion, documented as such). (2) the currentState/state split's reliance on 'each worklet frame arrives as its own platform-channel task' was a real but undocumented invariant; added an explicit doc comment on PearSwarm.state naming it. Reviewer also verified index.js's connectedCount/RECONNECTING logic against Hyperswarm's actual internal connection-swap guarantees (_connect's _allConnections check, _handleServerConnectionSwap's deferred promotion) rather than assuming it's race-free.
close_reason: Honest-failure state machine (discovering/connecting/connected/reconnecting/suspended-reserved/failed) implemented with a Dart-side bounded join timeout guaranteeing typed CONNECT_TIMEOUT failure instead of an infinite wait, plus the seventh spine failure-path test (write-after-close). 42 automated tests pass, reviewed independently with zero P1s (2 low-severity concerns found and fixed), and validated end-to-end on the Android emulator against the real bare-kit worklet.

title: E2.5: Session nonce + bundle-version attach handshake (T5)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, eng T5 + codex #6. RPC ids restart at 1 on hot restart (rpc.dart:34) with no session identity, so a stale worklet's late frames can be misattributed to the new Dart session. Native `started` boolean (FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt:~43) cannot carry identity across engine detach. Locked rule: reattach when healthy AND version-matched, else kill+restart.
FILES: rpc.dart, schema.dart/schema.js, pear-end/index.js, FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt, bare_worklet.dart, tests both packages.
STEPS:
1. On worklet start, pear-end generates a session nonce and reports {nonce, bundleVersion} in an attach frame (bundleVersion baked in at pack time by pack.dart).
2. Dart handshake on attach: store nonce; tag no frames (keep wire small) but REJECT inbound frames whose nonce field, when present on attach-scoped events, mismatches; simplest correct form: worklet includes nonce in the attach frame only, Dart drops ALL frames received before a valid attach and re-handshakes on restart.
3. Version check: Dart compares reported bundleVersion to its shipped pear-end version; mismatch -> control-channel kill + fresh start (never converse with a stale bundle).
4. Tests: mock attach with old version -> kill+restart invoked; frames before attach dropped; matched version -> reattach continues session.
VALIDATION: flutter test in flutter_pear and flutter_pear_bare green; on-device: hot restart with unchanged bundle reattaches; with a bumped bundle version restarts cleanly.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Attach frame carries nonce+bundleVersion; EVERY worklet frame carries the session nonce and mismatches are dropped (incl. post-attach arrival, tested); version mismatch triggers kill+restart, match reattaches; all behaviors unit-tested and device-verified across hot restart.
notes: Correction: the close reason has another shell-quoting artifact -- backticks around the word "static" triggered command substitution, dropping it from one sentence. Should read: "making PearRpc._nextId a `static` counter (my initial fix for cross-generation id-collision safety) does NOT survive a Dart hot restart". No functional impact -- scribe error only, same class of mistake as the earlier correction on flutter_pear-7be.3. Noting to self: never use backticks inside a double-quoted --reason/--notes string.
close_reason:
Implemented the session nonce + bundle-version attach handshake: pear-end generates a SESSION_NONCE (crypto.randomBytes) once at worklet boot and stamps it (envelope-level 'n' field, PearHandshakeField.envelopeNonce) on EVERY frame it sends. A new PearMethod.attachInfo request/response (deliberately NOT an event, so it answers correctly whether Dart is asking for the first time or re-asking after a hot-restart reattach to an already-running worklet) returns {nonce, bundleVersion}. Pear.start() always calls attach.info immediately after constructing PearRpc; on a bundleVersion mismatch against the generated kPearEndBundleVersion Dart constant, it disposes+terminates the worklet, boots a fresh one, and re-checks once -- if it STILL mismatches, throws PearException(BUNDLE_VERSION_MISMATCH) rather than silently proceeding. bundleVersion is a SHA-256 hash (truncated) of index.js + schema.js + package-lock.json, computed and written to both a gitignored pear-end/version.js (bundled in) and a committed lib/src/bundle_version.dart by a new pack.dart step -- content-based, not human-maintained, so ANY relevant change is auto-detected.

MAJOR DISCOVERY while validating this on-device: found and fixed a severe, PRE-EXISTING bug (present since E1.3) in flutter_pear_bare's core IPC transport -- BasicMessageChannel<ByteBuffer> + Flutter's BinaryCodec silently delivers EMPTY data to Dart on every native-to-Dart send on this Flutter/Android setup (matches the historical flutter/flutter#19849 engine bug class). Bisected conclusively: a hardcoded 5-byte buffer sent via BinaryCodec arrived as zero-length; the identical bytes sent via a fresh StandardMessageCodec channel arrived correctly. Every previous 'clean boot, no crash, Fully drawn' validation throughout this entire project (E1.3, E1.4, E2.1-E2.4, E4.1, E4.3) never actually proved a real byte round trip -- the M0 UI renders regardless of whether Pear.start() ever resolves, and nothing previously waited past the E2.2 RPC timeout to notice a hang. Fixed by switching flutter_pear_bare/ipc to StandardMessageCodec on both the Kotlin (FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt) and Dart (bare_worklet.dart) sides, sending/receiving plain ByteArray/Uint8List instead of raw ByteBuffer/ByteData. Retroactive correction notes added to flutter_pear-vyw.3, vyw.4, 7be.1-7be.4, 47k.1, 47k.3 acknowledging their on-device validation claims were unproven; their own Dart-level test/analyze validation remains valid and unaffected. Filed flutter_pear-5rl to track the real fix (device/emulator integration testing) so this class of bug can't recur silently.

REVIEW (independent agent, high-depth) caught a second real bug in my OWN first-draft fix: making PearRpc._nextId a  counter (my initial 'fix' for cross-generation id-collision safety) does NOT survive a Dart hot restart -- confirmed hot restart resets ALL static/isolate state while the NATIVE worklet keeps running with in-flight stale calls, and critically, on a same-worklet hot-restart-reattach (as opposed to a kill+restart) the envelope nonce is UNCHANGED too, so nonce-checking responses wouldn't have caught a collision either. Fixed by switching id generation to RANDOM (dart:math Random, checked for local non-collision against _pending) instead of any sequential scheme, static or not -- this closes the collision risk for BOTH the hot-restart-reattach and kill+restart scenarios uniformly, regardless of whether the nonce changed. Also fixed: bundleVersion's hash now includes package-lock.json (an npm dependency bump alone wouldn't have changed the version before); Pear.start() now wraps its whole worklet/rpc selection in try/catch so a failure fetching attach.info (e.g. timeout on a slow boot) disposes+terminates whatever was just constructed instead of leaking an orphaned worklet+PearRpc; added a one-line doc comment acknowledging the boot-time 'joining' lifecycle event is expected (harmless) to be dropped pre-session.

TESTS: schema_test.dart covers the 2 new constants + JS mirror cross-check. rpc_test.dart: _FakeWorklet now stamps a mutable sessionNonce on every frame; new tests for attach.info's response shape, a stale-generation event being dropped without side effects even post-session-establishment, and a response settling correctly regardless of nonce (protected by id uniqueness instead). 27/27 tests green (flutter_pear), 3/3 (flutter_pear_bare).

VALIDATION: melos run analyze clean (3/3); full test suites green; node --check clean; dart run flutter_pear:pack regenerates bundle+version files consistently. ON-DEVICE (the critical evidence, given what this ticket uncovered): with a CORRECT kPearEndBundleVersion, Pear.start() completes in seconds with no exception (confirmed via temporary debug instrumentation showing matches=true, since removed) -- verified with a 15s wait, well past the old 10s timeout that was masking the transport bug. With a DELIBERATELY WRONG kPearEndBundleVersion (Dart-only change, JS bundle unchanged), correctly detects the mismatch, kills+restarts, detects the same mismatch again, and throws PearException(BUNDLE_VERSION_MISMATCH) with the exact expected message in ~8 seconds. Both debug and release builds install and boot cleanly on the Android emulator across every iteration of this fix.

title: E2.6: Native crash observation feeding worklet.onCrash (T1)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, eng T1. LOCKED (codex #5): crash detection is NATIVE — a crashed worklet cannot serialize its own crash envelope, and crashes can predate IPC init. The Kotlin side observes Bare Kit worklet exit/error and reports over the CONTROL channel; Dart exposes worklet.onCrash and fails pending RPC calls.
FILES: FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt, bare_worklet.dart (add onCrash stream + state transition), rpc.dart (subscribe: fail pending on crash), tests both packages.
STEPS:
1. Kotlin: hook Bare Kit's worklet exit/uncaught-error callback (investigate what bare-kit exposes; if only process-level, monitor that); on fire, invoke a control-channel callback into Dart with {reason, detail}.
2. Dart BareWorklet: broadcast Stream<WorkletCrash> onCrash; state -> stopped; crash before first attach also surfaces (the pre-IPC case).
3. PearRpc subscribes: on crash, fail all pending completers with typed E_CRASHED (registry code) carrying the native reason.
4. Debug logging: crash logged loudly in debug mode (CLAUDE.md non-negotiable).
5. Tests: mocked control-channel crash event -> onCrash emits, pending call fails typed, state is stopped; crash-before-attach emits too.
VALIDATION: flutter test both packages green; on-device: kill the worklet (forced JS process exit via a test frame) -> onCrash fires with reason.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Kotlin observes worklet exit/error incl. pre-IPC and reports over control channel; onCrash stream emits typed reason; pending RPC calls fail with E_CRASHED; loud debug log; unit + device verified.
notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review found no P1s but 2 P2 gaps -- (1) bare_worklet_test.dart had zero coverage of the real onCrash/_onControlCall bridge (fixed, test added), (2) a JS self-reported crash arriving before ANY RPC response (the highest-risk boot-time crash window, since _currentNonce is still null then) was silently dropped by the ordinary event nonce gate, losing all diagnostic detail (fixed: workletCrash now bypasses the nonce gate ONLY while no session is established yet). A follow-up adversarial review of that specific nonce-gate change caught that my first attempt overclaimed safety (a comment said cross-generation delivery was impossible) -- it is NOT, because _ipc/_control are static channels shared across BareWorklet instances and Flutter buffers a channel message with no handler registered for delivery to whichever handler registers next, so a stale generation's delayed crash report could in principle reach a healthy new generation's PearRpc while its nonce is still null. Narrowed the fix accordingly: the exemption only applies while _currentNonce is null; once a session is established, workletCrash is gated exactly like any other event (new regression test added proving a stale-nonce crash event is dropped post-session, and the pending call it targeted is unaffected). Filed flutter_pear-3vh to track properly hardening the underlying shared-static-channel handoff risk (also present, pre-existing and unaddressed, in the native backstop's control channel) -- not blocking this close since the residual failure mode is a loud, recoverable false WORKLET_CRASHED report, not silent/corrupting, and requires two independent low-probability events to coincide. Also hardened BareWorklet._onControlCall with an idempotency guard (early-return if already stopped) as a defensive nit, even though no reachable double-fire path exists today.
close_reason: Dual-path crash observation (JS self-report + native IPC-ended backstop) implemented, tested (34+3 automated tests incl. 8 crash-specific cases), reviewed twice (implementation review + adversarial nonce-gate-safety review), and validated end-to-end on the Android emulator via debug.forceCrash. Follow-up hardening tracked in flutter_pear-3vh.

title: E2.4: 1-byte frame-type discriminator on the IPC envelope (T4)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, eng T4 + codex. rpc.dart:57-62 distinguishes frames by attempting JSON decode and SILENTLY returns on failure (line 59) — a raw binary frame would vanish. Locked: reserve a 1-byte discriminator NOW (0x00=JSON control, 0x01=raw binary); streaming MACHINERY stays out (bulk decision = file-path first, benchmark before any in-channel streaming).
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/rpc.dart, pear-end/index.js + schema.js, test/rpc_test.dart
STEPS:
1. Prepend the discriminator byte on every outbound frame (Dart _encode and pear-end send); strip+branch on inbound.
2. 0x00 -> existing JSON path. 0x01 -> loud stub: emit a diagnostic event / debug log, never silent-drop. Unknown byte -> same loud path.
3. Update E1's pear-end accordingly and rebuild the bundle.
4. Tests: JSON frame round-trips; 0x01 frame reaches the stub handler (observable), not swallowed; unknown byte observable; malformed JSON after 0x00 no longer silent (routes to the decode-failure path used by crash/diagnostics).
VALIDATION: flutter test test/rpc_test.dart green; on-device smoke with rebuilt bundle still chats.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Every frame carries 0x00/0x01 discriminator both directions; binary/unknown frames surface loudly instead of silent-dropping; JSON path unchanged for callers; wire change covered by tests and device smoke.
notes: RETROACTIVE CORRECTION (2026-07-03): this issue's on-device 'clean boot, no crash' checks never actually proved a real worklet->Dart byte round trip -- see flutter_pear-vyw.3 for the root cause (BinaryCodec platform-channel bug, present since E1.3, fixed in E2.5/flutter_pear-7be.5). The Dart-level unit tests and analyze/build validation for THIS issue's own logic remain valid and unaffected; only the on-device smoke-test claims are corrected.
close_reason:
Every IPC frame is now prefixed with a 1-byte PearFrameType discriminator (0x00=JSON control, 0x01=raw/reserved) on both sides. rpc.dart: _encode prepends the byte; _onFrame reads it first, and anything unexpected -- an unrecognized discriminator byte, malformed JSON after a valid 0x00, a well-formed JSON value that isn't an object, or a well-formed JSON object matching neither a response (numeric id) nor an event (string ev) -- now emits a new PearEventName.rpcDiagnostic event via the existing events stream instead of vanishing silently (the exact bug this ticket exists to fix: rpc.dart previously did 'catch (_) { return; }' with zero observability). pear-end/index.js mirrors all of this: send() prepends FrameType.JSON, IPC.on('data') branches on the discriminator and calls a new diagnostic() helper for the same unrecognized-shape cases.

Found and fixed two real bugs while implementing this:
1. Independent review caught that I'd applied the 'never silent-drop' loudification inconsistently even within the same function -- I loudified 'valid JSON, not an object' but left an equally-malformed adjacent case ('valid JSON object, but neither response nor event shape') silently falling through, on both the Dart AND JS sides. Extended the same diagnostic treatment to both for consistency (new test: 'a well-formed JSON object matching neither response nor event is surfaced').
2. Found while touching the exact line: pear-end's pre-existing 'if (typeof frame.id !== "number") return' would throw an uncaught TypeError if a request frame's JSON body were literally 'null' (JSON.parse('null') -> JS null -> null.id throws) -- a pre-existing crash risk (bare-kit aborts the whole host process on uncaught JS exceptions) unrelated to but immediately adjacent to this ticket's own change. Fixed with a '!frame ||' guard; verified the specific null-input case no longer throws via a standalone node -e reproduction (pear-end/index.js can't run under plain Node -- it depends on the BareKit global -- so this was checked in isolation, not by executing the real file).

TESTS (test/rpc_test.dart): _FakeWorklet.send() now asserts every outbound frame starts with PearFrameType.json (free coverage for every existing test), and respond()/new sendJsonFrame()/sendRawFrame() helpers construct properly-framed incoming frames. 5 new tests: a PearFrameType.raw frame surfaces as rpc.diagnostic; an unrecognized byte (0x7f) likewise; malformed JSON after a valid 0x00 likewise; a well-formed-but-wrong-shape JSON object likewise; a normal swarm.connection event still round-trips correctly through the discriminator-aware decode path. 25/25 tests green total (flutter_pear).

VALIDATION: melos run analyze clean (3/3); flutter test green (25/25); node --check passes on both pear-end/index.js and schema.js; dart run flutter_pear:pack regenerates the bundle; rebuilt + installed + launched on the Android emulator (via the subsequent E4.1 build) -- clean boot, no crash, 'Fully drawn', confirming the new discriminator-framed bundle boots fine in practice, not just in unit tests. Also confirmed via code reading that the M0 example app (which talks to the low-level BareWorklet API directly, bypassing PearRpc entirely) has its user-visible behavior completely unchanged by this diff -- its raw-text echo demo doesn't go through _encode/_onFrame at all.

title: E2.3: Route err.code to typed exception subtypes (T3)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, eng T3. rpc.dart:70 always throws base PearException; the subtypes (exceptions.dart:24 PearConnectionException, :31 PearStorageException) are never constructed. Errors must arrive TYPED so app devs can catch selectively.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/rpc.dart, exceptions.dart, schema.dart (err.code registry), test/rpc_test.dart
STEPS:
1. Define the err.code -> exception-subtype mapping keyed by code prefix or registry entry (explicit table in schema.dart, not string heuristics).
2. Route in _onFrame: construct the mapped subtype, preserving message/code/stack; unknown codes fall back to base PearException (never throw on unknown).
3. Tests: connection-class code -> PearConnectionException; storage-class -> PearStorageException; unknown -> base; stack preserved on all.
VALIDATION: flutter test test/rpc_test.dart green; analyze clean.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Registry-driven mapping constructs the right subtype with message/code/stack preserved; unknown codes fall back to base safely; each path unit-tested.
notes: RETROACTIVE CORRECTION (2026-07-03): this issue's on-device 'clean boot, no crash' checks never actually proved a real worklet->Dart byte round trip -- see flutter_pear-vyw.3 for the root cause (BinaryCodec platform-channel bug, present since E1.3, fixed in E2.5/flutter_pear-7be.5). The Dart-level unit tests and analyze/build validation for THIS issue's own logic remain valid and unaffected; only the on-device smoke-test claims are corrected.
close_reason:
Added an explicit err.code -> exception-subtype registry: PearErrorCategory enum (connection/storage) in schema.dart, PearErrorCode.categories (a const Map<String, PearErrorCategory> with exactly 2 entries today: unknownPeer->connection, storageUnavailable->storage -- LOCKED as explicit entries, no prefix/substring heuristics), and a new pearExceptionFor(message, {code, stack}) factory in exceptions.dart that looks up the code and constructs PearConnectionException/PearStorageException/base PearException accordingly via an exhaustive Dart 3 switch expression. All 3 PearException construction sites in rpc.dart (timeout, worklet-err response, dispose) now route through this factory for consistency.

Added PearErrorCode.storageUnavailable ('STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE') as a RESERVED code -- nothing throws it yet since no storage features exist (E5 not started), but it gives the registry a second real category to test ahead of E5 actually using it, same pattern as E2.1's reserved frame-type/handshake constants.

TESTS: 3 new cases in test/rpc_test.dart -- a connection-class code (PearErrorCode.unknownPeer) throws PearConnectionException with message/code/stack all preserved; a storage-class code (storageUnavailable) throws PearStorageException likewise; an arbitrary unregistered code string throws the base PearException specifically verified to NOT be either subtype (isNot(isA<...>()) x2), with its code preserved. 19/19 tests green total.

REVIEW (independent agent): no correctness bugs. Confirmed the registry lookup is a genuine compile-time const map with correct value-based String equality (verified via a real jsonEncode/jsonDecode round-trip in the test, not object identity), the switch expression is truly exhaustive, and -- importantly -- traced that PearErrorCode.unknownPeer is not just theoretically mapped but ACTUALLY thrown by pear-end/index.js's connection.write handler today using the exact same wire string, so PearConnectionException fires on a real, reachable worklet error path right now, not just in the simulated test. Two optional nits applied: added the missing  assertion to the storage-class test for parity with the connection-class one, and ran dart format on all touched files (fixed line-wrapping in exceptions.dart/rpc.dart/rpc_test.dart).

VALIDATION: melos run analyze clean (3/3 packages); flutter test green (19/19, flutter_pear); dart run flutter_pear:pack regenerates the bundle; rebuilt + installed + launched flutter_pear_example on the Android emulator -- clean boot, no crash, 'Fully drawn'.

title: E2.2: Per-call RPC timeout + fail-pending-on-death (T2)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, eng T2. PearRpc.call() (rpc.dart:43-53) parks a Completer that never times out — a dropped frame or dead worklet hangs the Future forever. Locked split: timeout catches HANGS here; crash DETECTION is native (separate task E2.5).
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/rpc.dart, packages/flutter_pear/test/rpc_test.dart (new)
STEPS:
1. Add a per-call timeout (default constant in schema.dart, overridable per call) that completes the pending Completer with a typed timeout PearException (err.code from the registry).
2. On dispose() and on worklet-death notification, fail ALL pending completers with typed errors (dispose already loops — give it the typed code).
3. Tests against a mock worklet: (a) never-answered call times out with the typed code, does not hang; (b) dispose fails pending calls; (c) late response after timeout is ignored without crash.
VALIDATION: cd packages/flutter_pear && flutter test test/rpc_test.dart green; analyze clean.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Unanswered call completes with typed timeout error within the bound; dispose/death fails all pending typed; late responses ignored safely; all three cases unit-tested.
notes: RETROACTIVE CORRECTION (2026-07-03): this issue's on-device 'clean boot, no crash' checks never actually proved a real worklet->Dart byte round trip -- see flutter_pear-vyw.3 for the root cause (BinaryCodec platform-channel bug, present since E1.3, fixed in E2.5/flutter_pear-7be.5). The Dart-level unit tests and analyze/build validation for THIS issue's own logic remain valid and unaffected; only the on-device smoke-test claims are corrected.
close_reason:
Added a per-call timeout to PearRpc.call() (schema.dart: PearRpcDefaults.callTimeout, default 10s, overridable per call) and centralized ALL completer resolution through one _settle(id, {result, error}) method -- the per-call Timer, _onFrame's real response handling, and dispose()'s fail-all loop each go through it, so exactly one of them ever resolves a given call and a late arrival after another already settled it is a silent no-op, not a double-complete crash. Introduced a minimal WorkletIpc interface (flutter_pear_bare) with just incoming/send so PearRpc depends on that instead of the concrete BareWorklet -- lets flutter_pear_test's planned in-memory fake (E3, already blocked on this) substitute for a real worklet in tests.

REVIEW (independent agent) caught two real gaps in the 'fail-pending-on-death' half of this ticket that I fixed before closing:
1. _worklet.send()'s Future was never awaited or error-handled -- if the worklet was already not-running at call() time, BareWorklet.send()'s StateError became an unhandled Zone error AND the call just sat for the full timeout before wrongly failing with RPC_TIMEOUT instead of a code reflecting what actually happened. Fixed: send()'s Future now has .catchError attached that immediately settles the call with a new PearErrorCode.sendFailed.
2. No dispose-guard on call() -- calling after dispose() registered a new completer+timer that would hang for the full timeout before failing. Fixed: a _disposed flag makes post-dispose calls fail immediately with PearErrorCode.workletDisposed via Future.error(), never touching _worklet.send at all.

TESTS (test/rpc_test.dart, new file): 11 cases -- normal ok/err responses, unanswered call times out with RPC_TIMEOUT, dispose fails all pending with WORKLET_DISPOSED, late response after timeout/after dispose both silently ignored (not a crash), send failure settles immediately with SEND_FAILED, call() after dispose() fails immediately without ever reaching send(). All fast (no test takes >20ms of simulated delay).

Also caught and fixed two bugs in my OWN test-writing during this: a dispose test that awaited dispose() BEFORE attaching expectLater() (Dart reports an error-completed Future with no listener yet as unhandled -- fixed by attaching the expectation first); and a 'late response after dispose' test that forgot to actually call dispose() in its own body (relied on tearDown, which runs too late) -- fixed by disposing explicitly inline.

VALIDATION: melos run analyze clean (3/3); flutter test green (19/19, flutter_pear); flutter_pear_bare's own test suite still green (WorkletIpc/@override additions are purely additive, confirmed no BareWorklet behavior change); dart run flutter_pear:pack regenerates the bundle; rebuilt + installed + launched flutter_pear_example on the Android emulator after every change in this ticket -- clean boot each time, no crash, 'Fully drawn'.

close_reason:
Authored packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/schema.dart (Dart constants: PearMethod, PearEventName, PearErrorCode, PearFrameType [reserved], PearHandshakeField [reserved]) and pear-end/schema.js (hand-kept JS mirror, no codegen per LOCKED decision).
